reviews.summary: This paper proposes Difficulty-Calibrated Unlearning Auditing (DCUA), a framework that uses reference-model difficulty scores to stratify forget-set samples into quintiles for per-stratum MIA evaluation. It introduces WQ-AUC and Difficulty Gap metrics, and investigates DAU, a difficulty-aware loss reweighting defense. The core idea of bringing difficulty calibration from the MIA literature into unlearning evaluation is sensible and timely given recent concerns about aggregate metrics.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed 5+ distinct online searches. The paper properly positions itself relative to key related work: Hayes et al. (2025) on per-example U-MIAs, Watson et al. (2022) on difficulty calibration for MIAs, Zhao et al. (2024) on RUM partitioning, and Rizwan et al. (2024) on instance-level unlearning difficulty. The specific contribution of bridging difficulty calibration into unlearning evaluation with reference-model-based stratification is novel. The DAU defense is a natural extension but shows mostly negative results. No substantially similar method was found in my search.
strengths: The core idea of using difficulty calibration for stratified unlearning evaluation is well-motivated and addresses a real gap identified in recent literature.
The DCUA framework is computationally efficient compared to per-example shadow model approaches (O(K) vs O(n)).
The negative results for DAU on most methods (except Random Labels) are honestly reported and constitute useful scientific knowledge.
The paper includes ablation studies for reference model count K and stratification granularity.
Experiment code exists and appears to implement the described methods.
weaknesses: CRITICAL: Results in the paper do not match the results.json file. For example, CIFAR-10 RL DG is 0.260 in Table 1 but 0.034 in results.json.
CRITICAL: The abstract claims 'All 15 method--dataset configurations show statistically significant overestimation (p < 0.005)' but results.json shows only 10/15 WQ>Agg and 11/15 DG>0 significant.
CRITICAL: Fabricated citations. cheng2026toward cites a 2026 publication (impossible), and sepahvand2025leveraging cites ICML 2025 (conference hasn't occurred).
Purchase-100 overestimation values as low as 0.006 fall outside the claimed 0.08-0.35 range.
Only 3 random seeds provides limited statistical power.
Some DAU results have NaN p-values (SCRUB/Purchase).
No error bars shown in figures.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper's core contribution is timely and sensible, but serious integrity issues prevent acceptance. First, the numbers in Table 1 do not match the results.json file. The discrepancy for CIFAR-10 Random Labels is particularly severe (DG of 0.260 claimed vs 0.034 actual). Second, the statistical significance claims are overstated - the abstract claims all 15 configurations significant at p<0.005, but only 10/15 show significant WQ>Agg differences. Third, there are fabricated citations with impossible future dates (cheng2026toward with year 2026, sepahvand2025leveraging for ICML 2025 which hasn't occurred). These are not minor issues - they indicate the research is not trustworthy. To make this paper acceptable, the authors would need to: (1) correct all table values to match actual experimental results, (2) correct statistical claims to reflect actual significance levels, (3) verify and correct all citations, (4) include more seeds for better statistical power, and (5) include proper error bars and confidence intervals.
questions_for_authors: Why do the values in Table 1 not match the results.json file? For example, CIFAR-10 RL shows DG=0.034 in results.json but DG=0.260 in the paper.
How do you explain the claim that 'All 15 configurations show statistically significant overestimation' when results.json shows only 10/15 WQ>Agg tests significant?
Can you verify the existence and correct citations for cheng2026toward and sepahvand2025leveraging? These appear to have incorrect publication dates.
What explains the NaN p-value for SCRUB/Purchase100 DAU test?
Were the figures generated from results.json or from a different data source?
reviews.integrity_check: FAIL. Major integrity issues identified: (1) Table 1 values in the paper do not match results.json - for example, CIFAR-10 Random Labels DG is 0.260 in paper but 0.034 in results.json. (2) Abstract claims all 15 configurations significant at p<0.005 but results.json shows only 10/15 WQ>Agg significant. (3) Fabricated citations with future dates: cheng2026toward (year 2026) and sepahvand2025leveraging (ICML 2025, which hasn't occurred). These are grounds for automatic rejection per the reviewer guidelines.

reviews.summary: The paper argues that aggregate membership-inference metrics can hide severe per-sample privacy failures in machine unlearning, and proposes Difficulty-Calibrated Unlearning Auditing (DCUA), which stratifies forgotten samples by reference-model difficulty and reports worst-quintile AUC and a difficulty gap. It also studies a difficulty-aware reweighting defense (DAU) across several unlearning methods and datasets, positioning the defense results as mostly negative except for Random Labels.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed multiple online searches covering the exact title, the core technique/domain combination, related cited work, method-component combinations, and recent venue proceedings. Exact-title searches did not reveal an existing paper with the same title. However, the central scientific claim is already substantially anticipated by recent work: Hayes et al. ("Inexact Unlearning Needs More Careful Evaluations to Avoid a False Sense of Privacy") already shows that aggregate unlearning MIAs can be misleading and advocates per-example evaluation; Naderloui et al. (RULI) also pushes toward finer-grained, per-sample privacy auditing; Zhao et al. ("What Makes Unlearning Hard and What to Do About It") studies difficulty heterogeneity in unlearning; and Watson et al. provides the difficulty-calibration mechanism borrowed here. Recent searches over NeurIPS/OpenReview/arXiv did not show an identical "difficulty-calibrated quintile auditing" protocol, so the combination appears somewhat new, but it is best viewed as an incremental repackaging of already-emerging ideas rather than a clear conceptual advance. Under the stated reviewer policy, this keeps novelty below the accept bar.
strengths: The paper targets a real evaluation problem in machine unlearning: average-case privacy metrics can miss vulnerable subsets, and that question is important for deployment.
The manuscript is generally well organized, and the proposed summary metrics (worst-quintile AUC and difficulty gap) are easy to understand.
The workspace contains substantial code, generated artifacts, and experiment outputs, so this is not a submission with missing experimental materials.
weaknesses: The paper fails the required integrity check: many numbers in paper.tex do not match results.json. For example, the paper reports CIFAR-10 Random Labels as Agg/WQ/DG = 0.592/0.753/0.260 and Purchase-100 Random Labels as 0.426/0.775/0.657, while results.json gives 0.4428/0.4628/0.0342 and 0.2502/0.3520/0.3485 respectively.

Several global claims in the manuscript are contradicted by results.json. The paper claims all 15 method-dataset cases have significant WQ > Aggregate and DG > 0, but results.json reports only 10/15 significant for WQ > Aggregate and 11/15 for DG.

The method description does not match the implementation. The paper says it uses a calibrated loss-threshold MIA, but the code computes three attacks and reports the maximum AUC via run_all_attacks/best_auc. Likewise, the appendix describes much milder unlearning hyperparameters than the actual run_v3.py pipeline.

The model specification is inconsistent with the code and results metadata. The paper/results.json describe Purchase-100 as using an MLP (1024-512-256-100), while exp/shared/models.py implements 600->512->256->128->100.

The claimed defense conclusions are not trustworthy because the manuscript is stale relative to the experiment outputs and logs. The logs also show multiple incompatible experiment versions, which makes it unclear which run truly supports the text.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The main issue is trustworthiness, not merely polish. Before this work can be evaluated scientifically, the authors need to regenerate the paper directly from one authoritative result source and ensure every table, figure, statistical statement, and hyperparameter description matches the released code and results.json. In particular, the manuscript should be updated to reflect the actual attack used in code, the actual Purchase-100 model architecture, and the actual significance outcomes. It would also help to separate the diagnostic contribution from the exploratory defense contribution more cleanly, since the novelty over Hayes/RULI/Zhao is modest and currently rests mostly on a particular stratification scheme. Once the integrity issues are fixed, the authors should more carefully position DCUA as an efficient approximation to existing fine-grained auditing rather than as a fundamentally new discovery that aggregate metrics can mislead.
questions_for_authors: Which artifact is the authoritative source for the reported results: paper.tex, results.json, exp/results_v3/final_results.json, or the run_v3 logs?
Why does the paper state that the MIA method is the calibrated loss-threshold attack, while the released code reports the maximum over loss-threshold, calibrated, and LiRA-style attacks?
Why do the unlearning hyperparameters and SCRUB/GA descriptions in the paper differ substantially from run_v3.py?
Can you provide a corrected manuscript automatically generated from the released result files so that every reported number can be verified?
reviews.integrity_check: Experiment code is present and the figure-generation scripts appear to use saved result files, so the workspace is not empty. However, the submission is not trustworthy in its current form: paper.tex contains many table entries and statistical claims that contradict results.json, the method description does not match the actual code, and the logs show multiple inconsistent experiment versions. Under the stated guidelines, this is an automatic rejection.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Difficulty-Calibrated Unlearning Auditing (DCUA), a framework that stratifies forget-set samples into difficulty quintiles using reference-model calibration to expose per-sample privacy gaps in machine unlearning evaluation. It introduces WQ-AUC and Difficulty Gap metrics and investigates a Difficulty-Aware Unlearning (DAU) defense, finding it largely ineffective except for Random Labels unlearning.
reviews.novelty_assessment: Online searches reveal substantial overlap with concurrent and prior work. Hayes et al. (SaTML 2025) already demonstrated that per-example U-MIAs reveal privacy failures masked by aggregate metrics. Cheng et al. (2026) proposed circuit-level difficulty metrics that explicitly enable stratifying results by difficulty range. Naderloui et al. (USENIX Security 2025, RULI) introduced per-sample targeted attack frameworks. Sepahvand et al. (ICML 2025) linked per-instance privacy to data difficulty. Wang & Cheng (USENIX Security 2025) proposed sample-level unlearning completeness measures. Zhao et al. (NeurIPS 2024, RUM) already partitions forget sets by difficulty. The specific metric names WQ-AUC and Difficulty Gap are new, but the conceptual contribution of difficulty-stratified unlearning evaluation is well-covered by existing work. The paper acknowledges Hayes et al. as closest work but understates the overlap with CUD, RULI, and Sepahvand et al. The claimed computational advantage over Hayes et al. (O(K) vs O(n)) is real but incremental. All 12 cited references were verified as real publications.
strengths: Clear problem formulation: the paper articulates the gap between aggregate and per-sample unlearning evaluation well, with a clean mathematical framework (WQ-AUC, DG metrics).

Honest negative results: the DAU defense largely fails, and the paper reports this transparently rather than cherry-picking successes. The comparison with RUM showing both defenses fail is informative.
Comprehensive experimental design: 5 unlearning methods × 3 datasets × 3 seeds with multiple ablation studies (K, granularity, alpha, forget set size, random-weight control).
All cited references are real and verifiable publications — no hallucinated citations.
Well-written paper with clear notation, good figures, and transparent discussion of limitations including the Purchase-100 synthetic dataset issue.
weaknesses: Critical results integrity issue: Paper table numbers systematically differ from results.json. For example, CIFAR-10 GA WQ-AUC is reported as 0.792 in the paper but 0.7875 in results.json; SCRUB-DAU CIFAR-10 WQ-AUC is 0.796 in the paper but 0.854 in results.json — a difference of 0.058. This is not a rounding issue; the numbers are fundamentally different, suggesting the paper may have been written from different experimental runs than the provided results.json.

Limited novelty given concurrent work: The core idea of evaluating unlearning quality stratified by sample difficulty is already addressed by Hayes et al. (per-example U-MIA), Cheng et al. (CUD circuit-level metric with difficulty stratification), Naderloui et al. (RULI per-sample targeted attacks), and Sepahvand et al. (per-instance DP for unlearning). The paper's contribution is essentially applying Watson et al.'s difficulty calibration to the unlearning domain — a straightforward transfer.

The DAU defense, which consumes roughly half the paper, is largely a negative result. While informative, this weakens the overall contribution since the evaluation-only contribution (DCUA) is incremental over existing work.

Only one MIA method (calibrated loss-threshold) is used for the main evaluation despite the paper mentioning three in the config. The simplified LiRA implementation (z-score based) deviates from the original Gaussian likelihood ratio approach.
Purchase-100 is a synthetic proxy dataset, and the retrained model already shows DG=0.162 (should be ~0), suggesting distributional issues that compromise results on this dataset.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper addresses a real and important problem but arrives somewhat late to the party. The most critical issue is the mismatch between paper tables and results.json: nearly every number in Table 1 differs from what results.json reports (e.g., CIFAR-10 GA Agg is 0.597 in paper vs 0.602 in results.json; WQ is 0.792 vs 0.788; DG is 0.304 vs 0.293). The DAU table discrepancies are even larger (SCRUB-DAU CIFAR-10 WQ: 0.796 vs 0.854). This must be resolved — either the paper was written from an earlier experimental run and results.json was updated, or there is a more serious integrity concern. For revision: (1) ensure all paper numbers exactly match the authoritative results file, (2) substantially strengthen the related work discussion to include CUD, RULI, per-instance DP unlearning, and sample-level unlearning completeness work, (3) provide a clearer articulation of what DCUA adds beyond applying Watson et al.'s calibration to the unlearning setting, (4) consider evaluating with the actual LiRA attack and additional MIA methods to strengthen the evaluation, and (5) replace the synthetic Purchase-100 with a real tabular dataset or at least diagnose why retrained models show non-zero difficulty gaps.
questions_for_authors: Why do the numbers in the paper tables not match results.json? For instance, CIFAR-10 SCRUB-DAU WQ-AUC is 0.796 in the paper but 0.854 in results.json. Which is correct?
How does DCUA compare to CUD (Cheng et al., 2026), which also assigns per-sample difficulty scores and enables stratified evaluation? What does DCUA capture that CUD does not?

The experiment logs show the pipeline was restarted multiple times (v1, v2, v3) with different hyperparameters. Which run produced the final reported results, and were hyperparameters tuned on the test metrics?
Why does the retrained model on Purchase-100 show DG=0.162, significantly above zero? Does this indicate a flaw in the synthetic dataset construction or the evaluation methodology?

Have you considered stratifying by other difficulty proxies (e.g., memorization score, influence function magnitude) to validate that the reference-model approach is the best stratification mechanism?
reviews.integrity_check: Experiment source code exists and implements the described methods (DCUA stratification, DAU reweighting, all 5 unlearning algorithms). Logs show actual training runs with realistic timings (e.g., 159s for fine-tuning, 5-20s for gradient ascent). However, there is a MAJOR integrity concern: the numbers reported in the paper tables systematically differ from results.json. CIFAR-10 values differ by 0.005-0.015 in most cells, and SCRUB-DAU CIFAR-10 WQ differs by 0.058. The experiment log also shows multiple pipeline restarts with different hyperparameter calibrations, suggesting results may have been cherry-picked across runs. All cited references are verified as real publications.
